concepts.name: Werner's Theory of Coordination Compounds
concepts.explanation: Werner proposed that metals exhibit two types of valences: primary (ionisable, satisfied by negative ions) and secondary (non-ionisable, fixed for a metal, equal to coordination number). The secondary valences have specific spatial arrangements (coordination polyhedra).

concepts.name: Coordination Entity and Central Atom/Ion
concepts.explanation: A coordination entity consists of a central metal atom/ion bonded to a fixed number of ions or molecules. The central atom/ion is the species to which ligands are attached. Examples: [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺ (central atom Co³⁺), [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ (central atom Fe²⁺).

concepts.name: Ligands – Unidentate, Didentate, Polydentate, and Chelating
concepts.explanation: Ligands are ions or molecules bound to the central atom. Unidentate ligands bind through one donor atom (e.g., Cl⁻, NH₃, H₂O). Didentate ligands bind through two donor atoms (e.g., en, C₂O₄²⁻). Polydentate ligands have multiple donor atoms (e.g., EDTA⁴⁻, hexadentate). Chelating ligands form ring structures with the metal.

concepts.name: Ambidentate Ligands
concepts.explanation: Ambidentate ligands have two different donor atoms and can bind through either one. Examples: NO₂⁻ can bind through N (nitrito-N) or O (nitrito-O); SCN⁻ can bind through S (thiocyanato-S) or N (thiocyanato-N).

concepts.name: Coordination Number (CN)
concepts.explanation: The coordination number is the number of ligand donor atoms directly bonded to the central metal ion. Only sigma bonds are counted, not pi bonds. Examples: [PtCl₆]²⁻ has CN = 6; [Ni(NH₃)₄]²⁺ has CN = 4; [Fe(C₂O₄)₃]³⁻ has CN = 6 (oxalate is didentate).

concepts.name: Coordination Sphere and Counter Ions
concepts.explanation: The coordination sphere consists of the central atom/ion and the ligands attached to it, enclosed in square brackets. Counter ions are outside the brackets and balance the charge. Example: in K₄[Fe(CN)₆], [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ is the coordination sphere, and K⁺ are counter ions.

concepts.name: Coordination Polyhedra – Octahedral, Tetrahedral, Square Planar
concepts.explanation: The spatial arrangement of ligand atoms around the central atom defines the coordination polyhedron. Common shapes: octahedral (CN=6, e.g., [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺), tetrahedral (CN=4, e.g., [Ni(CO)₄]), and square planar (CN=4, e.g., [PtCl₄]²⁻).

concepts.name: Oxidation Number of Central Atom
concepts.explanation: The oxidation number is the charge the central atom would carry if all ligands were removed along with the electron pairs shared with it. It is written as a Roman numeral in parentheses after the name. Example: [Cu(CN)₄]³⁻ has Cu(I); [Co(NH₃)₅Cl]²⁺ has Co(III).

concepts.name: Homoleptic and Heteroleptic Complexes
concepts.explanation: Homoleptic complexes have only one type of donor group (e.g., [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺). Heteroleptic complexes have more than one type of donor group (e.g., [Co(NH₃)₄Cl₂]⁺).

concepts.name: Double Salts vs Coordination Compounds
concepts.explanation: Double salts (e.g., Mohr's salt, potash alum) dissociate completely into simple ions in aqueous solution. Coordination compounds (complexes) do not dissociate into simple ions; the complex ion remains intact. Example: K₄[Fe(CN)₆] does not give Fe²⁺ and CN⁻ ions.

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds – Rules for Writing Formulas
concepts.explanation: Rules: (1) Central atom is listed first; (2) Ligands are listed in alphabetical order (irrespective of charge); (3) Polydentate ligands are enclosed in parentheses; (4) The entire coordination entity is enclosed in square brackets; (5) Charge is indicated outside brackets as a superscript.

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds – Rules for Naming
concepts.explanation: Rules: (1) Cation is named first; (2) Ligands are named in alphabetical order before the central atom; (3) Anionic ligands end in -o (e.g., chloro, cyano); (4) Prefixes di, tri, etc., indicate the number of ligands; (5) Oxidation state is given as Roman numeral in parentheses; (6) Metal name ends with -ate for anionic complexes.

concepts.name: Geometrical Isomerism in Coordination Compounds
concepts.explanation: Geometrical isomerism arises in heteroleptic complexes due to different spatial arrangements of ligands. Examples: cis-trans in square planar [MX₂L₂] and octahedral [MX₂L₄]; fac-mer in octahedral [Ma₃b₃]. Tetrahedral complexes do not show geometrical isomerism.

concepts.name: Optical Isomerism in Coordination Compounds
concepts.explanation: Optical isomers (enantiomers) are non-superimposable mirror images. They rotate plane-polarised light in opposite directions (d and l). Common in octahedral complexes with didentate ligands, e.g., [Co(en)₃]³⁺. Only the cis-isomer of [PtCl₂(en)₂]²⁺ shows optical activity.

concepts.name: Linkage Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Linkage isomerism arises when an ambidentate ligand can coordinate through different donor atoms. Example: [Co(NH₃)₅(NO₂)]Cl₂ has two isomers – nitrito-N (yellow, bound through N) and nitrito-O (red, bound through O).

concepts.name: Coordination Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Coordination isomerism arises from the interchange of ligands between cationic and anionic entities of different metal ions. Example: [Co(NH₃)₆][Cr(CN)₆] and [Cr(NH₃)₆][Co(CN)₆] are coordination isomers.

concepts.name: Ionisation Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Ionisation isomerism arises when the counter ion is itself a potential ligand and can displace a ligand to become the counter ion. Example: [Co(NH₃)₅SO₄]Br and [Co(NH₃)₅Br]SO₄ are ionisation isomers.

concepts.name: Solvate (Hydrate) Isomerism
concepts.explanation: Solvate isomerism arises when a solvent molecule is either directly bonded to the metal or present as a free solvent molecule in the crystal lattice. Example: [Cr(H₂O)₆]Cl₃ (violet) and [Cr(H₂O)₅Cl]Cl₂·H₂O (grey-green) are hydrate isomers.

concepts.name: Valence Bond Theory (VBT) in Coordination Compounds
concepts.explanation: VBT explains bonding in complexes through hybridisation of metal orbitals. The metal uses (n-1)d, ns, np orbitals (inner orbital complexes, low spin) or ns, np, nd orbitals (outer orbital complexes, high spin) to form hybrid orbitals that accept electron pairs from ligands. Examples: [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺ (d²sp³, inner orbital), [CoF₆]³⁻ (sp³d², outer orbital).

concepts.name: Inner Orbital and Outer Orbital Complexes
concepts.explanation: Inner orbital complexes (low spin) use inner d-orbitals (e.g., d²sp³), resulting in maximum pairing of electrons. Outer orbital complexes (high spin) use outer d-orbitals (e.g., sp³d²), resulting in less pairing. Examples: [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ (inner orbital, low spin) vs [FeF₆]³⁻ (outer orbital, high spin).

concepts.name: Magnetic Properties from VBT
concepts.explanation: VBT explains magnetic behaviour: complexes with unpaired electrons are paramagnetic; those with all electrons paired are diamagnetic. Magnetic moment (μ) is calculated using the spin-only formula: μ = √[n(n+2)] BM, where n is the number of unpaired electrons.

concepts.name: Limitations of Valence Bond Theory
concepts.explanation: Limitations: (1) Involves many assumptions; (2) Does not give quantitative interpretation of magnetic data; (3) Cannot explain the colour of coordination compounds; (4) Does not distinguish between weak and strong ligands; (5) Does not predict tetrahedral vs square planar structures accurately.

concepts.name: Crystal Field Theory (CFT) – Introduction
concepts.explanation: CFT is an electrostatic model that treats the metal-ligand bond as purely ionic (electrostatic interactions). Ligands are treated as point charges (anions) or point dipoles (neutral molecules). It explains the splitting of d-orbital energies in a crystal field.

concepts.name: Crystal Field Splitting in Octahedral Complexes
concepts.explanation: In an octahedral field, the d orbitals split into two sets: t₂g (lower energy: dxy, dyz, dxz) and e_g (higher energy: dx²-y², dz²). The energy difference between them is Δ₀ (octahedral crystal field splitting energy). The magnitude of Δ₀ determines whether the complex is high spin or low spin.

concepts.name: Crystal Field Splitting in Tetrahedral Complexes
concepts.explanation: In a tetrahedral field, the d orbital splitting is inverted compared to octahedral: e (lower energy: dx²-y², dz²) and t₂ (higher energy: dxy, dyz, dxz). Δ_t = (4/9)Δ₀. Because Δ_t is smaller, low spin configurations are rarely observed in tetrahedral complexes.

concepts.name: Spectrochemical Series
concepts.explanation: The spectrochemical series is the order of ligands according to their ability to split d-orbital energies (Δ₀). Strong field ligands (high Δ₀) cause pairing of electrons; weak field ligands (low Δ₀) do not. Order: I⁻ < Br⁻ < Cl⁻ < F⁻ < OH⁻ < H₂O < NH₃ < en < CN⁻ < CO.

concepts.name: High Spin and Low Spin Complexes
concepts.explanation: High spin complexes have maximum unpaired electrons (weak field ligands, small Δ₀). Low spin complexes have minimum unpaired electrons (strong field ligands, large Δ₀). Example: [Fe(H₂O)₆]³⁺ (high spin, 5 unpaired electrons) vs [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ (low spin, 1 unpaired electron).

concepts.name: Colour of Coordination Compounds (d-d Transitions)
concepts.explanation: Colour arises from d-d transitions: an electron from a lower energy d orbital (t₂g) is excited to a higher energy d orbital (e_g). The energy of the absorbed light corresponds to Δ₀. The observed colour is complementary to the absorbed colour. Example: [Ti(H₂O)₆]³⁺ appears violet (absorbs blue-green).

concepts.name: Crystal Field Stabilisation Energy (CFSE)
concepts.explanation: CFSE is the energy stabilisation gained by the d electrons due to crystal field splitting. It is calculated by considering the number of electrons in t₂g and e_g orbitals: CFSE = (-0.4 × n_t₂g + 0.6 × n_e_g) × Δ₀ (in octahedral field).

concepts.name: Limitations of Crystal Field Theory
concepts.explanation: Limitations: (1) Assumes purely ionic bonding (ignores covalent character); (2) Treats ligands as point charges (not accurate for neutral ligands); (3) Cannot explain the spectrochemical series quantitatively; (4) Does not explain the partial covalent character of metal-ligand bonds.

concepts.name: Metal Carbonyls – Bonding (Synergic Effect)
concepts.explanation: Metal carbonyls have both σ and π bonding. The M-C σ bond is formed by donation of lone pair from CO's carbon to a vacant metal orbital. The M-C π bond is formed by back-donation from a filled metal d orbital to the vacant π* orbital of CO. This synergic bonding strengthens the metal-carbon bond.

concepts.name: Applications of Coordination Compounds – Analytical Chemistry
concepts.explanation: Coordination compounds are used in qualitative and quantitative analysis. Examples: EDTA is used for estimating hardness of water (Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ complexes); DMG (dimethylglyoxime) is used for the detection of Ni²⁺; cupron is used for Cu²⁺ detection.

concepts.name: Applications of Coordination Compounds – Metallurgy
concepts.explanation: Coordination compounds are used in extraction and purification of metals. Examples: Gold is extracted using cyanide (formation of [Au(CN)₂]⁻); nickel is purified via the formation and decomposition of [Ni(CO)₄] (Mond's process).

concepts.name: Applications of Coordination Compounds – Biological Systems
concepts.explanation: Coordination compounds are vital in biological systems. Examples: Chlorophyll (Mg complex) for photosynthesis; Haemoglobin (Fe complex) for oxygen transport; Vitamin B₁₂ (Co complex) for red blood cell formation; Carbonic anhydrase (Zn complex) as an enzyme.

concepts.name: Applications of Coordination Compounds – Medicinal Chemistry
concepts.explanation: Coordination compounds are used in medicine. Examples: cis-platin (Pt complex) for cancer treatment; EDTA for lead poisoning (chelate therapy); D-penicillamine for removing excess copper (Wilson's disease).

concepts.name: Applications of Coordination Compounds – Catalysis and Industry
concepts.explanation: Coordination compounds are used as catalysts. Examples: Wilkinson's catalyst [(Ph₃P)₃RhCl] for hydrogenation of alkenes; Ziegler-Natta catalysts (TiCl₄ + AlR₃) for polymerisation of ethylene. They are also used in electroplating (Ag and Au from cyanide complexes) and photography (hypo dissolves AgBr via [Ag(S₂O₃)₂]³⁻).

concepts.name: Chelate Effect
concepts.explanation: The chelate effect is the enhanced stability of complexes containing chelating (polydentate) ligands compared to those containing unidentate ligands. This is due to the favourable entropy change (more particles are released) and the formation of stable five- or six-membered rings.
